ECE 484 · Principles of Safe Autonomy

F1TENTH Time Trial Challenge:
Reactive Racing Pipeline for Fast and Safe Autonomous Driving

Our goal is to complete a 3-lap autonomous time trial as quickly and reliably as possible. Using onboard LiDAR, reactive free-space reasoning, and an optimized Pure Pursuit controller, we aim to reduce crash penalties and manual rescues while preserving smooth, high-speed navigation.

[Your Name] · [Teammate 1] · [Teammate 2] · [Teammate 3]
University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign · Spring 2026
3-Lap Goal Finish the time trial with consistent, penalty-free autonomous performance.
LiDAR-Driven Use local perception and reactive gap reasoning without hardware modifications.
ROS 2 Pipeline Closed-loop data flow from /scan and /odom to Ackermann drive commands.
Watch Simulator Demo System Block Diagram Task Allocation

F1TENTH Autonomous Racing - ECE 484

Race Strategy

Project Overview & Approach

To tackle the F1TENTH Time Trial without changing hardware, we focus on a software-first racing stack. Our system uses LiDAR-based reactive navigation to identify traversable free space, select safe and efficient local targets, and generate smooth steering and speed commands through Pure Pursuit-based control.

01
Fast local perception Continuously process scan data to understand track structure and obstacle proximity in real time.
02
Gap-aware target selection Choose feasible local goals that balance safety, forward progress, and stability during turns.
03
Penalty reduction Minimize collisions and rescues by improving robustness in narrow or high-curvature track segments.

Preliminary Demo

Simulator Validation

We have successfully configured the F1TENTH ROS 2 simulation environment. The video below should demonstrate the initial vehicle setup, occupancy grid map, LiDAR scans in RViz2, and the first closed-loop validation of motion and sensing.

[Embed your RViz2 / Simulator Demo Video Here]

Replace this box with your screen recording, YouTube iframe, or MP4 preview.

Suggested content: spawn scene, teleop or initial autonomous motion, LiDAR visualization, and track-following behavior.

System Design

ROS 2 Closed-Loop Architecture

Our system is organized as a clean closed-loop pipeline: sensor topics provide local scene information, perception modules infer free space, goal-generation logic selects an actionable target, and the controller converts this target into Ackermann steering and speed commands.

/scan + /odom LiDAR and vehicle state input
Preprocessing Filter noise and structure useful local observations
Gap Detection Identify safe free-space candidates
Target Generation Select local waypoint or target heading
Pure Pursuit Compute steering and speed commands
/drive Ackermann output to simulator or car
[Insert your polished block diagram here]

Keep the arrows and module names consistent with the pipeline above.

Figure 1. End-to-end data flow from local sensing to final control commands, with future deployment onto the Jetson-based physical platform.

Core Logic

Method Breakdown

1. LiDAR Processing

  • Filter invalid range values and optionally constrain the field of view.
  • Stabilize scan interpretation for local decision-making.
  • Prepare structured observations for downstream free-space analysis.

2. Gap Detection

  • Detect candidate traversable regions from local scan geometry.
  • Measure angular width, clearance, and relative safety of each gap.
  • Reject weak candidates that could increase crash risk.

3. Goal Generation

  • Select a target direction or local waypoint from the chosen gap.
  • Bias the choice toward forward progress and smoother future motion.
  • Reduce oscillation through practical heuristics and filtering.

4. Pure Pursuit Control

  • Convert local targets into Ackermann steering commands.
  • Adjust speed according to curvature, stability, or risk level.
  • Maintain smooth closed-loop behavior across straightaways and turns.
Execution Plan

Team Plan & Task Allocation

To move efficiently from simulator validation to real vehicle deployment, the project is divided into tightly coupled technical tracks.

Perception

[Teammate 1 Name]

  • Focus: LiDAR processing and gap detection
  • Filter scan noise and build robust free-space interpretation.
  • Implement detection logic for safe, traversable local gaps.
Control

[Teammate 2 Name]

  • Focus: Pure Pursuit and speed policy
  • Transform local targets into stable steering outputs.
  • Tune control behavior for faster and smoother lap performance.
Localization

[Teammate 3 Name]

  • Focus: Odometry, TF, and environment understanding
  • Maintain correct frame relationships and motion context.
  • Support future track-aware improvements if mapping is introduced.
Integration

[Teammate 4 Name]

  • Focus: ROS 2 integration and hardware deployment
  • Ensure node communication and stable system bring-up.
  • Lead the simulator-to-Jetson deployment path for Milestone 3.
